title: Spend Before June 30? The Cash Timing Calculation Most SME Owners Skip
linkedin:
Cash leaves in June. The tax benefit arrives months later. Most EOFY purchase decisions treat them as the same moment, and that is where the cash pressure comes from.

Every year from May onward, equipment suppliers run EOFY campaigns, vendors extend terms, and business owners field calls about what to buy or prepay before June 30. Tax deductions from asset purchases are real, and year-end is a natural time to make commitments.

What gets skipped more often than it should is a short calculation that changes the decision significantly.

When a business settles a significant invoice before June 30, the cash leaves on that day. For most Australian businesses using a tax agent, the income tax return for the year ending June 30 is lodged between October and May of the following year. The reduction in tax payable arrives after that.

A $40,000 purchase means $40,000 leaves in June. The tax saving is a fraction of that amount and it materialises months later.

The gap falls in July and August, when several pressures arrive at once. Superannuation for the April to June quarter is due by 28 July. New financial year revenue is still building. Prior year collections have already come in.

The question worth asking is not whether the deduction makes sense. It is what the cash position looks like in the second week of July after the purchase.

A 13-week cash flow forecast projected into September makes that picture visible before the commitment is made.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

Here's a question worth pausing on before June 30: when you make that big equipment or software purchase, when does the cash actually leave, and when does the tax benefit arrive?

The answer, for most Australian businesses, is that the cash goes in June and the tax saving lands months later, once the return is lodged and processed.

That timing gap is what creates the cash pressure in July and August, when super obligations, slower new-year revenue, and reduced working capital all land together.

Have you run the numbers on what your July looks like with that purchase in it?

google:
EOFY spending decisions look very different once you add cash timing to the tax calculation.

When a business settles a significant purchase before June 30, whether that is equipment, technology, or prepaid expenses, the full cost leaves the account on the day the payment is made. The tax benefit arrives later, once the income tax return for the financial year is lodged and processed. For most Australian businesses using a tax agent, that is somewhere between October and May of the following year.

The business carries the full cash cost through July and August, two months that already carry their own pressures. Superannuation for the April to June quarter is due by 28 July. New financial year revenue is still building. And any major purchase made in June reduces the opening working capital balance before the new year gains momentum.

The right question before any material EOFY commitment is not whether the deduction makes sense. It is what the July and August cash position looks like with that commitment in it.

ProfitPulse works with owner-led businesses in Brisbane, Queensland, and across the East Coast to build the forward cash view that makes this calculation straightforward before June 30, not painful in August.
